title: Time Series Analysis for Journalists
description: Master time series analysis through real journalism case studies. Diagnose your level and learn techniques for analyzing trends in news, politics, and media.

nodes.title: Diagnostic: Time Series Fundamentals in News
nodes.content: Test your understanding of time series concepts in journalism. These questions assess whether you're ready for advanced analysis techniques or need to build foundational skills first.

questions.question: A political news outlet tracks daily article traffic on election coverage. Over 4 years, they notice traffic spikes dramatically every November during midterms and presidential elections. What pattern is this?
feedback: A) Trends show sustained direction over months/years.
B) Correct! Seasonality is a repeating pattern at predictable intervals.
C) Anomalies are unexpected one-time events.
D) Noise is random variation, not a predictable cycle.
questions.explanation: Seasonality is a repeating pattern that occurs at regular, predictable intervals. Election cycles happen on a fixed schedule, causing predictable spikes in political news readership.

questions.question: You're analyzing housing prices mentioned in real estate reporting over 3 years. Prices consistently increase from $250K to $425K. What are you observing?
feedback: A) Seasonality repeats regularly; this is sustained direction.
B) Anomalies are brief unexpected spikes.
C) Correct! A trend is a long-term directional movement.
D) Lag effect is historical correlation, not a directional pattern.
questions.explanation: A trend is the overall direction a time series moves over an extended period. The sustained increase from $250K to $425K shows a strong upward trend in housing markets.

questions.question: A financial journalist tracking oil prices notices they normally fluctuate between $70–90 per barrel. During a Middle East conflict, prices spike to $130 in 48 hours. What term describes this unexpected jump?
feedback: A) Trends develop gradually over weeks/months.
B) Seasonality is regular and predictable; geopolitical shocks aren't.
C) Correct! An anomaly is an unexpected deviation from normal patterns.
D) Seasonality doesn't shift—it's the repeating baseline pattern.
questions.explanation: An anomaly is an unusual, unexpected event that disrupts normal patterns. The sudden price spike caused by geopolitical conflict is an anomalous deviation from typical pricing.

nodes.title: Advanced: Decomposition & Forecasting for Data Journalists
nodes.content: Master advanced time series techniques for rigorous data journalism. Decomposition separates a time series into trend (long-term direction), seasonality (repeating cycles), and residual components—revealing the true drivers of change in your story. Learn to forecast future values using ARIMA models, enabling predictive reporting: predict election turnout, anticipate market swings, or forecast social trends. Detect anomalies automatically to spot emerging stories before they break elsewhere. These techniques add statistical credibility to complex narratives about change over time.

nodes.title: Case Study: Forecasting Election Engagement and Breaking News
nodes.content: An election correspondent analyzed their newsroom's article volume from 2016–2024, breaking the pattern into components: a 4-year seasonal cycle (more coverage in election years) and an upward trend (growing political polarization increasing coverage needs). Using ARIMA forecasting, they predicted coverage demand 3 months ahead, helping plan staffing. When anomalies appeared—like an unexpected primary upset—the model flagged it: "This event generated 40% more coverage than our forecast." This quantified the story's magnitude, proving it was historically significant. The forecasts also revealed that pre-election coverage peaks 6 weeks before Election Day, allowing them to pitch explanatory pieces precisely when reader interest peaks.

nodes.title: Advanced Time Series Mastery Complete
nodes.content: Excellent work! You've mastered advanced time series decomposition and forecasting. You can now separate trends from cycles, predict future patterns, and quantify anomalies. Use these skills to identify data-driven stories, anticipate what readers will care about, and add statistical rigor to change-over-time investigations.

nodes.title: Foundations: Recognizing Patterns in News Data
nodes.content: Time series data is measurements tracked over time—a fundamental tool in journalism. You encounter time series constantly: daily website traffic, weekly stock prices, monthly unemployment rates, or yearly violent crime statistics. Three patterns appear repeatedly. A trend is the overall direction: readership increasing, unemployment falling, or home prices rising. Seasonality is a repeating cycle: holiday shopping peaks, summer crime rises, or flu cases spike each winter. An anomaly is an unexpected disruption: a viral story shatters normal traffic, a sudden merger shocks stock prices, or a policy change causes a sudden drop in statistics. Identifying these patterns helps you explain change to readers and spot emerging stories.

nodes.title: Example: Tracking Social Media Engagement During a News Cycle
nodes.content: A local reporter broke a corruption story about the city council. Her social media follower engagement was normally 1,200 interactions daily. The day the story broke, engagement spiked to 8,900—an anomaly: an unexpected jump from major news. Over the next week, engagement slowly returned to baseline with a slight increase (now 1,450 daily)—showing a trend: the story permanently boosted her audience. Six months later, she noticed engagement was always higher on weekdays and dropped on weekends—seasonality. Understanding these patterns helped her pitch follow-up investigations when weekday engagement was highest and schedule threads that explained complex financial fraud on Monday mornings when readers were most engaged.

nodes.title: Practice: Identify Patterns in Journalism Data
nodes.content: Apply what you've learned by identifying trends, seasonality, and anomalies in real journalism scenarios.

questions.question: A climate reporter tracks extreme weather events in her region for 5 years. Summer months consistently have more wildfires, fall has hurricanes, and winter has ice storms. What pattern explains this?
feedback: A) A trend is sustained direction, not cycling.
B) Correct! Seasonality is a yearly repeating pattern tied to weather.
C) Anomalies are one-time surprises.
D) Correlation measures relationships, not temporal patterns.
questions.explanation: Seasonality is a repeating pattern at regular intervals. Weather events occurring predictably by season is the clearest example of seasonality.

questions.question: You're analyzing crime statistics for your beat from 2015–2025. Overall, serious crime declined from 8,200 incidents yearly to 4,100 incidents yearly—a gradual improvement. What does this represent?
feedback: A) Seasonality repeats; this is one-directional change.
B) Anomalies are surprises, not sustained patterns.
C) Correct! This sustained decline over years is a downward trend.
D) Cycles repeat; this doesn't—it's sustained improvement.
questions.explanation: A trend is a sustained, directional movement. The decline from 8,200 to 4,100 incidents over 10 years shows a strong downward trend in crime.

questions.question: A sports reporter covers a college football team. Ticket sales are normally 35,000 per game. The week they unexpectedly beat the #1 ranked team, ticket sales for the next game jump to 52,000. What is this jump?
feedback: A) Trends develop gradually over weeks/months.
B) Seasonality repeats predictably; upsets aren't scheduled.
C) Correct! An unexpected spike from a surprising event is an anomaly.
D) Volatility is general variability, not a specific term for spikes.
questions.explanation: An anomaly is an unexpected deviation from normal patterns. The unexpected surge in ticket sales caused by beating a top team is an anomalous spike.

nodes.title: Time Series Foundations Mastered
nodes.content: Well done! You now understand trends, seasonality, and anomalies—the core patterns in time series analysis. Apply these insights to your beat: track key metrics over time, recognize recurring patterns, and explain what changes mean to your readers. Start your next story by asking: Is this a trend, a seasonal cycle, or an unexpected anomaly?
